content: AS diagnosis: physical exam shows crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids, delayed carotid upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus), soft/absent A2, S4 gallop. Echocardiography is primary diagnostic tool: transthoracic echo assesses valve morphology, gradients, valve area (continuity equation), LV function, concomitant valve disease. Doppler measurements: peak jet velocity, mean gradient, dimensionless index (LVOT VTI / AV VTI; <0.25 suggests severe AS). Exercise testing useful for asymptomatic severe AS to elicit symptoms or abnormal BP response. Cardiac catheterization if discordance between clinical and echo findings or before noncardiac surgery. Biomarkers: elevated BNP predicts worse outcomes. CT aortic valve calcium scoring: Agatston score >2000 men or >1200 women indicates severe AS; useful for low-flow low-gradient cases. Cardiac MRI for LV mass, fibrosis assessment. Regular surveillance: mild AS every 3-5 years, moderate every 1-2 years, severe every 6-12 months or if symptoms change.
